title: Enabling Deterministic Passive Quantum State Transfer with Giant Atoms
authors: Oliver Diekmann, Enrico Di Benedetto, Nicolas Jungwirth, Daniele De Bernardis, Zeyu Kuang, Francesco Ciccarello, Stefan Rotter, Peter Rabl, Alejandro González-Tudela, Carlos Gonzalez-Ballestero
abstract: Achieving quantum state transfer in passive ways can become a powerful asset for scalable quantum networks. Here, we demonstrate how giant atoms coupled to 1D waveguides provide a platform for such a passive, deterministic transfer. Engineering the position and strength of coupling points, we show that the nonlocal interaction can be utilized for the emission of time-reversal-symmetric single-photon wavepackets by spontaneous decay. We first derive general analytical conditions under which arbitrary qubit decays can be mapped to wavevector-dependent couplings that guarantee perfect state transfer in the continuum limit of infinitely many coupling points. Then, for experimentally relevant configurations with a finite number of coupling points, we demonstrate that high transfer fidelities can still be achieved by optimization, reaching 87% with only two coupling points and exceeding 99% with ten or more. We further analyze the robustness of the protocol against disorder in leg positioning and extend the formalism to environments with nonlinear dispersion, showing that dispersion-induced distortions can be fully compensated by judiciously chosen setups. Our results establish giant atoms as a powerful platform for realizing high-fidelity quantum state transfer in a setting without time-dependent control, opening new avenues for scalable quantum networks and engineered light-matter interfaces.
summary: The paper shows how 'giant atoms' can be used to achieve high-fidelity, passive quantum state transfer by engineering their spatial coupling to create symmetric photon wavepackets. This method works even in the presence of nonlinear dispersion and is robust against experimental disorder, offering a scalable approach for quantum networks.
main_problem: Achieving deterministic, passive quantum state transfer between distant qubits in a quantum network without the need for time-dependent active control pulses.
main_result: Demonstrated that giant atoms (emitters with multiple coupling points) can be engineered to emit time-reversal-symmetric wavepackets, enabling high-fidelity state transfer (exceeding 99% with 10+ legs) and robustness against dispersion and disorder.
method: Used a resolvent operator approach and Laplace transforms to solve the inverse problem of mapping desired qubit decay profiles to wavevector-dependent couplings, combined with numerical optimization of leg positions and strengths.
model_or_system: Two giant atoms coupled to a 1D waveguide (or Coupled Cavity Array) with non-local, chiral, or bidirectional coupling, modeled via a Hamiltonian including wavevector-dependent coupling strengths.
key_observables: Excited-state populations, transfer fidelity (P2,max), wavepacket envelope, and pulse shape mismatch.
important_parameters: Number of coupling points (N), coupling strengths, leg positions, decay timescale (tau), and dispersion nonlinearity.
main_assumptions: Initially assumes chiral coupling (unidirectional) and linear dispersion, though the paper extends to bidirectional and nonlinear cases.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Schematic of giant atoms and evolution of populations; Fig 2: Qubit populations and spatial coupling for different decay types; Fig 3: Scaling of fidelity with the number of legs and pulse shapes; Fig 4: Experimental platforms (SAWs/CCAs) and robustness to positional disorder; Fig 5: Implementation of chiral emission via phase engineering; Fig 6: Optimization of pulse shape mismatch for Gaussian vs. exponential decays.
paper_structure: Introduction to the problem of passive transfer, derivation of analytical conditions in the continuous limit, optimization for finite-leg configurations, analysis of robustness against disorder and nonlinear dispersion, extension to chiral engineering in non-chiral baths, and discussion of experimental platforms.
why_interesting: It provides a blueprint for scalable quantum networks by replacing active, time-dependent control with static, engineered spatial structures, directly relevant to waveguide QED and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Passive quantum state transfer between giant atoms chirally coupled to a waveguide with a linear dispersion. (a) Two giant atoms are coupled to the waveguide at N discrete coupling points x(α) j , g(α) j (j = 1, ..., N; α = 1, 2) (see text for details). The nonlocal coupling allows the left qubit to emit an almost time-reversal-symmetric pulse (blue), that can be absorbed by the right qubit. (b) The interference of contributions from different coupling points gives rise to a wavevector-dependent coupling g1(k). (c) Resulting evolution of the excited-state populations |cα(t)|2 of the two qubits. The configuration assumed in this figure is the result of the optimization in Fig. 3...

Figure 2. Quantum state transfer with wavevector-dependent couplings. (a) Populations of the qubits for an exponential decay where we set γ = 2/τ. (b) Spatial coupling configura- tion (orange, solid lines for positive, dashed lines for negative values) which is real for the present cases after demodulating with eikqx. This coupling profile leads to the emission of the associated time-reversal-symmetric single-photon pulse (blue, shifted to the right for better visibility). Panels (c,d) are anal- ogous to panels (a,b) but for a Gaussian decay [see Eq. (14)]. The configurations in panels (b) and (d) will also work for a bidirectional coupling, in that case granting chiral emission.
Figure 3. (a) State transfer infidelity as a function of the number of legs. We optimize the leg positions x(1) i and coupling strengths g(1) i under the constraint π P

Figure 4. (a) Potential platforms to implement the transfer protocol using giant atoms coupled to a slow-light waveguide implemented in a cavity array (left) or surface acoustic waves (right). (b) Sensitivity against random deviations of the leg positions with respect to the optimal configuration. The optimized leg positions of Fig. 3 are displaced by Gaussian noise of width σ = λq/20 (orange) and σ = λq/10 (blue). The violin plot illustrates the resulting distributions for the transfer fidelity P2 = maxt|c2(t)|2, where the vertical bar indicates the mean of the respective distribution. (c) Spatial coupling distributions for perfect chiral state transfer for a Gaussian decay and the curved...

Figure 5. Directional emission for quantum state transfer by doubling the number of legs. (a) Setup of a giant atom coupled at equidistant points in a window of 5 vgτ with coupling strengths sampled from Eq. (A2). After sampling 10 points, they can be made real-valued by shifts on the wavelength scales [see Eq. (A3)]. Directionality with only two different phases is then achieved by doubling the number of points according to Eq. (A1). The height of the bars indicates coupling strength and the color indicates the coupling phase. Bars are shifted to the left or right of their respective coupling point for better visibility.
